title: LSP overhead: pool per-repo, skip for short roles, daemonize serena
description:
## Observed cost (2026-05-09)
With 5 active specialist worktrees on a single dev box, 82 LSP
processes were running consuming ~5.3 GB combined RSS. Per-worktree
breakdown:
- debugger role: ~12 LSPs (serena uvx + serena python + tsserver×2 +
  pyright×2 + node wrappers)
- executor role: ~8 LSPs (similar but lighter)

5 active specialists ≈ 50 LSPs ≈ 4 GB just for language tooling.

## Three independent improvements (in priority order)

### A. LSP pool per repo, not per worktree (BIGGEST WIN)
Today: each worktree of the same repo launches its own LSP stack. So 5
worktrees of xtrm-tools = 5 separate tsserver processes, 5 pyrights, 5
serenas — even though they share node_modules / .venv.

Proposal: specialists keeps a per-repo LSP daemon. Worktree clients
connect via a tiny adapter that forwards LSP requests to the shared
daemon, scoping requests by worktree path. LSP protocol's
workspace/folders support handles multi-folder fan-out within a server.

Impact: 5 worktrees × 10 LSPs/repo collapse to 1 worktree × 10
LSPs/repo = 10 LSPs total. Savings: ~3 GB on a 5-worktree session.

Risk: complex. Requires understanding each LSP's workspace state model;
some servers don't gracefully handle multi-workspace concurrent edits.
Start with serena (custom, easier to control) before tackling
tsserver/pyright.

### B. Skip LSPs for short / read-only roles (CHEAPEST WIN)
researcher, code-sanity, security-auditor, sync-docs and similar roles
mostly read files. They don't need a live LSP — symbolic operations
could be one-shot via `tsc --noEmit` style probes or omitted entirely.

Today every role spawns the full LSP stack at startup regardless.

Proposal: per-role config flag (lsp: full | minimal | none). Default
debugger=full, executor=full, researcher=minimal, code-sanity=none.
Saves ~150-300 MB per non-debugger specialist invocation.

Risk: low. Roles already declare their tool needs; adds one config knob.

### C. Serena daemon mode (UPSTREAM FEATURE REQUEST)
Each specialist invocation spawns:
  uv tool uvx --from git+https://github.com/oraios/serena serena start-mcp-server --transport streamable-http --port <random> --project <path> --context agent

Cost: fresh Python interpreter + dependency import + project indexing
every time (~80-200 MB resident, ~2-5 sec startup).

Proposal: long-lived serena daemon (one per machine) accepting new
project mounts on the fly. Specialists framework just attaches its
desired project to the daemon and gets a session ID back.

Requires upstream PR to oraios/serena. Worth filing even if specialists
doesn't implement it themselves — Mercury could fund the feature.

Impact: 30+ serena procs (each ~80 MB) collapse to 1 (~150 MB).
Savings: ~2.4 GB on a busy session.

## Why P0
Memory pressure forced a manual cleanup session today. Available RAM
hit 1.3 GB (out of 31 GB) with swap fully saturated; questdb baseline
+ active LSPs squeezed everything else. Each new specialist run
incrementally pushes the box closer to OOM kills.

## Cross-refs
- unitAI-ctl0o (P1 in this repo): gitnexus mcp leaks via --keep-alive.
  Same root cause family: specialists framework spawns heavyweight
  child processes without coordinated lifecycle/pooling.
- infra-xcd (in mercury/infra): shared Dolt server migration. Same
  spirit — N processes -> 1 daemon for state servers.

## Suggested phasing
Phase 1: Implement (B) — config flag for LSP intensity per role. Cheap.
Phase 2: Implement (A) — per-repo LSP pool. Most impact, most work.
Phase 3: Open upstream PR (C) for serena daemon mode.

title: Epic: shared LSP/Serena gateway — IDE-like single server per branch, cache substrate, gzrx route enforcement
description:
## PROBLEM
Today every specialist with MEDIUM/HIGH permission spawns its own
Serena MCP + LSP child via pi-mcp-adapter. With ~30 spawns/hour during
active sessions this produces:
- Cold-start cost per specialist (LSP indexing, npm install in worktree)
- RAM churn (each LSP holds its own type graph)
- Token waste (every specialist re-discovers symbols already indexed)
- Per-worktree leak surface (gitnexus and dolt experience same family —
  see unitAI-ctl0o, unitAI-0wz2p)

The structural fix is the IDE pattern: a long-lived language-server per
project, owned by a session-managing gateway, queried by all clients.
Specialists become read-clients of a shared substrate, not LSP launchers.

## SUCCESS
- Single Serena MCP daemon process per branch (not per specialist),
  alive across multiple consecutive specialist runs in the same branch.
- Specialists' direct Serena access denied via gzrx; semantic queries
  routed through a code-intel-gateway capability.
- Persistent SQLite cache for documentSymbol / workspaceSymbol /
  definition / references / outline, keyed by file content hash, valid
  across specialist invocations.
- Tree-sitter / ast-grep exposed as first-class tier-2 tool for
  structural query/rewrite (the cheap ~80% case).
- Measured: RAM-time per specialist drops materially; cache hit ratio
  observable; LSP cold-start eliminated for second+ run on same branch.

## SCOPE
The epic owns the runtime architecture for code intelligence. Targets:
- src/specialist/ runtime (where pi extensions are loaded)
- .specialists/catalog/ + manifest-resolver (gzrx tier policy)
- New: src/specialist/code-intel-gateway/ (daemon + session pool +
  cache)
- New: src/specialist/code-intel-client/ (the surface specialists call)
- pi-mcp-adapter glue (route Serena calls through gateway)

## NON_GOALS
- SCIP / LSIF precomputed indexes (deferred — optimization, not
  structural fix).
- New language coverage. Same set as today (TS/JS/Python primarily).
- Replacing gitnexus. gitnexus stays the front-door for graph/recall
  queries; this epic is the semantic-oracle tier behind it.
- Replacing Serena. Serena stays as the LSP wrapper; this epic shares
  it across specialists instead of spawning per-job.

## CONSTRAINTS
- Cache key: `repo_id + branch + tool-config-hash` (tsconfig.json /
  pyproject.toml / go.mod). NOT worktree_path — multiple worktrees on
  the same branch share the LSP session. (Counter-intuitive but it's
  how IDEs handle multi-window-on-same-project.)
- Daemon transport: UDS not file-mediated (avoids the bd post-checkout
  class of bugs we just hit in unitAI-0wz2p — git hooks scaffolding
  state inside worktrees).
- Lifecycle: start-on-first-connect, idle-evict after N minutes,
  RAM-pressure eviction; NOT 'alive forever'.
- gzrx integration: hard-deny direct `serena.*` at runtime tier when
  the gateway capability is healthy; auto-fallback to direct Serena if
  gateway is degraded (matches existing extension-health protocol).

## VALIDATION
- Soak: 10 consecutive specialist runs on same branch — second through
  tenth show no LSP cold-start, sub-100ms documentSymbol responses
  from cache, single Serena process visible in `ps -ef`.
- gzrx parity: direct `mcp__serena__find_symbol` from a test
  specialist returns 'tool not allowed', while `mcp__code_intel__*`
  proxies to the gateway successfully.
- Telemetry: cache hit/miss counters surface in `sp result` metrics
  block per specialist run.
- No regression in existing serena-cheatsheet tests (mandatory rule
  template_set).

## OUTPUT
A multi-child epic plan when this is broken down. The head issue
captures intent + constraints. Detailed child decomposition deferred to
a planner pass when the operator promotes this from backlog.

## ARCHITECTURE SKETCH (sequenced; details deferred to child beads)
1. Design decision (overthinker): gateway protocol (UDS+JSON-RPC vs
   MCP-over-stdio vs HTTP), cache key composition, eviction policy.
2. `code-intel-gateway` daemon scaffold: single Serena process, UDS
   server, session pool, start-on-connect + idle-TTL.
3. SQLite cache layer: schema, file-hash invalidation, capability
   coverage.
4. gzrx policy: deny direct Serena, route via gateway capability;
   manifest-resolver change + per-tier deny rules.
5. Specialist-side `code_intel.*` tool surface: proxy capability that
   publishes documentSymbol/workspaceSymbol/definition/references/etc.
6. (Parallel) Tree-sitter / ast-grep first-class tool for structural
   tier-2 query/rewrite — independent of gateway, ships in parallel.
7. Soak + telemetry: RAM-time per specialist, cache hit ratio, gateway
   uptime per branch.
8. Migration: flip default from per-specialist Serena spawn to
   gateway-routed; deprecate old path with one-release window.

## CROSS-REFS
- unitAI-c4g0m — original 'LSP overhead' P0; this epic is the
  structural answer
- unitAI-ctl0o, unitAI-0wz2p — same lifecycle-failure family (every-
  job-spawns-its-own-server pattern)
- existing gzrx machinery: docs/design/gzrx-tool-catalog.md,
  src/specialist/manifest-resolver.ts (the runtime extension-policy
  resolver this epic plugs into)
- gitnexus front-door pattern (already in place for graph queries; this
  epic adds the LSP-tier behind it)

## DECISION OPEN
This is the head issue. Do not start implementation from this bead
alone. Promote to active by spawning a planner pass to decompose into
file-disjoint children with explicit dependencies.

title: Epic: production-harden script/service specialists and evaluate SDK runner
description:
## Overview

Harden script-class specialists (`sp script` and `sp serve`) for production non-coding pipelines such as darth-feedor article/news summarization, then evaluate and roll out a Pi SDK-backed runner if benchmarks prove it better than per-request Pi subprocesses.

This epic is ordered for production recovery first: small subprocess hardening fixes land before trust cleanup, readiness improvements, and SDK migration work.

## Goals

- Resume darth-feedor production pipeline ASAP with deterministic prompt handling and bounded failure modes.
- Keep script/service specialists usable as non-coding LLM transforms with no coding tools by default.
- Preserve Pi auth/model compatibility while reducing prompt leakage, process-list leakage, and runtime drift.
- Establish an SDK migration path behind a flag, with subprocess fallback until proven safe.

## Non-goals

- Do not change package-class `sp run` specialist orchestration semantics.
- Do not enable coding tools for service/script specialists.
- Do not require darth-feedor to clone specialists source or change its HTTP contract unless explicitly versioned.

## Success criteria

- Production-fast hardening tasks are complete before SDK work begins.
- `sp script`/`sp serve` prompts are not passed via argv and run with correct cwd/project context.
- Prompt size, output schema, trust flags, db path, and readiness behavior are test-covered.
- SDK runner is benchmarked against subprocess runner on darth-feedor-style article workloads before becoming default.

## Context / background

- Discovered from specialists-6vy: Pi CLI auto-context injection required isolation flags for script-class calls.
- Relevant code: `src/specialist/script-runner.ts`, `src/cli/script.ts`, `src/cli/serve.ts`, docs under `docs/specialists-service*.md`, skill docs under `config/skills/using-script-specialists/`.
- Pi docs reviewed: CLI JSON mode, stdin in print/json mode, SDK `createAgentSession`, zero-tool/custom ResourceLoader possibilities, RPC mode tradeoffs.

Scope: triage all remaining open items, close stale bookkeeping-only work, park long-tail items, and split real implementation work from board drift. Hard exclusion: do not touch unitAI-smjsq reserved surfaces or dispatch implementation into specialist/config/runtime ownership files while smjsq is active. Goal: progressively work through rest of board in safe batches until only intentional active work remains.
notes: Progress update (2026-04-24): board-only Stage 1 triage completed. Applied deferred status to long-tail non-urgent items outside smjsq protected surfaces. Closed stale placeholder item specialist:debugger. Active keep-open set remains focused on real bugs/current work: explorer note-append bug, research-node descendants, task-scoped architecture snapshot injection, observability retention, sync-docs runtime budget guard, explicit git-root cwd fix, output_type surfacing.

title: Workflow registry entry: security-audit
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `security-audit` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when work touches auth, secrets, input handling, dependency lockfiles, agent/MCP/config surfaces, token storage, permissions, or when the user explicitly asks for a security audit.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. security-auditor READ_ONLY/LOW scan with bounded scope.
2. executor/debugger fix bead only for confirmed findings that require changes.
3. reviewer validates fixes against security-auditor findings.
4. Optional cross-cutting security-auditor pass on cumulative integration diff before publication.

Relationship edges:
- `validates` from security-auditor and reviewer beads to implementation/fix bead.
- `discovered-from` for follow-up hardening work.
- `caused-by` when a concrete vulnerability/failure is traced to a root-cause bead.
- `until` for time/event-bounded security preconditions that gate release.
- Do not encode advisory findings as `blocks` unless they are hard release blockers.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows suggest` must prefer this workflow for sensitive-surface language and include scan-only constraints in the generated bead contract.

Pitfalls
========
Do not let security-auditor edit files or run destructive/live exploit scans. Do not skip the pass on sensitive surfaces because the diff is small.

title: Workflow registry entry: test-generation
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `test-generation` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when the task asks to add or improve tests, create regression coverage, classify failing suites, or attach test coverage to a new implementation plan.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. test-planning for strategy and coverage target when new tests are needed.
2. executor only for writing tests when scope is concrete.
3. test-runner for executing commands and classifying failures.
4. reviewer for non-trivial test diff or when tests gate production behavior.

Relationship edges:
- `validates` from test-runner/reviewer bead to implementation bead or test-generation bead.
- `caused-by` from failing-test bead to root-cause bead in failure-map flows.
- `discovered-from` for missing coverage or product bugs found while writing tests.
- `blocks` only when implementation cannot be considered complete before test evidence lands.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows plan` must separate test planning, test writing, and test execution/classification; executor should not own full-suite validation.

Pitfalls
========
Do not ask executor specialists to run full Vitest/Bun suites as their main validation path in this project. Use test-runner/reviewer for test execution and interpretation.

title: Implement sp workflows CLI and registry
description:
Purpose
=======
Implement the `sp workflows` command and canonical workflow registry.

Scope
=====
- `src/workflows/catalog.ts` (or equivalent registry module)
- `src/cli/workflows.ts`
- command registration/help in `src/index.ts` / CLI help surface
- tests for list/show/suggest/plan JSON behavior
- docs/skill cross-reference update

Command surface
===============
Minimum:

  sp workflows list
  sp workflows show <workflow-id>
  sp workflows suggest --bead <id>
  sp workflows suggest --prompt "<task description>"
  sp workflows plan --bead <id> --json

Later/guarded mutation:

  sp workflows apply --bead <id> --dry-run
  sp workflows apply --bead <id>

Success
=======
- Workflow registry is data-driven and covers all workflow entry beads under unitAI-ylphl.
- Human output is copy/paste useful.
- JSON output includes workflow ID, confidence, reasons, specialist sequence, bead topology, relationship edge types, commands, termination criteria, and pitfalls.
- `suggest` and `plan` do not mutate bead state.
- Help text includes `sp workflows`.

Relationship vocabulary
=======================
The command must emit typed relationships from unitAI-ylphl.8: `blocks`, `tracks`, `related`, `parent-child`, `discovered-from`, `until`, `caused-by`, `validates`, `relates-to`, `supersedes`, plus dedicated duplicate/supersede commands where appropriate.

title: Workflow registry entry: standalone-review
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `standalone-review` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when existing work already exists as a branch, PR, or completed specialist job and the task is review/verdict rather than implementation.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. reviewer `--bead <review-id> --job <existing-exec-job> --keep-alive` when a job/worktree exists.
2. reviewer against branch/PR metadata when no specialist job exists.
3. Optional code-sanity/security-auditor before reviewer for high-risk diffs.
4. Rebuttal/resume loop for one evidence-backed challenge, then escalate on second FAIL.

Relationship edges:
- `validates` from review bead to implementation/job bead.
- `discovered-from` for follow-up work found in review.
- `duplicate`/`supersedes` when review discovers this branch duplicates or replaces another tracked bead.
- `relates-to` for sibling PRs or conflict-cluster branches.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows suggest --bead <review>` should prefer this workflow when bead language says review, audit diff, PR, existing branch, or completed job.

Pitfalls
========
Launching reviewer without `--job` loses workspace context. Do not use `--prompt` for tracked review when a bead/job exists. Keep review bead distinct from implementation bead and link with `validates`.

title: Workflow registry entry: doc-sync
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `doc-sync` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use after code/config behavior changes when exactly one doc is stale or a bead explicitly scopes one doc for synchronization.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. session/report/source excerpt gathered before dispatch when needed.
2. sync-docs with one bead per doc and SCOPE naming exactly one doc.
3. reviewer only when doc sync is high-impact or policy-sensitive.
4. close doc bead after drift evidence is recorded.

Relationship edges:
- `validates` from doc-review/drift-check bead to doc-sync bead when used.
- `discovered-from` for additional stale docs found during sync.
- `relates-to` from doc-sync bead to implementation bead that caused drift.
- `blocks` only if docs must land before release/publication.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows plan` must enforce single-doc invariant and refuse whole-tree doc sync plans.

Pitfalls
========
Do not run sync-docs whole-tree. Do not edit source code in sync-docs workflow. Do not bury additional stale docs in notes; create follow-up beads with `discovered-from`.

title: Workflow registry entry: new-feature
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `new-feature` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use for greenfield or additive feature work, especially when scope spans more than two files, more than three steps, or requires test/doc planning.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. planner or planning skill creates epic/children and file ownership split.
2. test-planning attaches test AC gates or test beads.
3. executor per implementation child, parallel only for disjoint file scopes.
4. code-sanity/security-auditor advisory passes when triggers apply.
5. reviewer per implementation chain.
6. `bd dep cycles`, `sp epic status`, then `sp epic merge` for batch publication.

Relationship edges:
- `parent-child` via `bd create --parent <epic>` for child workflow beads.
- `blocks` for true sequencing between planner/test-plan/implementation when required.
- `validates` from reviewer/test/security/sanity beads to implementation beads.
- `relates-to` for conflict-cluster siblings that overlap but should not block each other yet.
- `discovered-from` for follow-up enhancements discovered by reviewer or test-runner.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows plan --bead <feature>` should output a child-bead skeleton, dependency edges, specialist sequence, and merge checklist.

Pitfalls
========
Do not skip planning when scope is broad. Do not launch parallel executors on the same file unless planning explicitly chooses restitch. Do not skip test-planning at issue creation time.

title: Workflow registry entry: refactor
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `refactor` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when behavior should be preserved but structure, naming, extraction, or module boundaries change. Use especially when blast radius is non-trivial.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. overthinker/design or explorer READ_ONLY pass to bound impact and invariants.
2. GitNexus impact/rename dry-run requirements in the bead contract.
3. executor implements refactor in a worktree.
4. code-sanity mandatory for complexity/type-safety risk; security-auditor if config/input/auth surfaces are touched.
5. reviewer validates behavior preservation and migration safety.
6. finalize + merge after PASS.

Relationship edges:
- `blocks` for impact/design pass before implementation when required.
- `validates` from reviewer/advisory/test beads to refactor bead.
- `supersedes` when a safer refactor bead replaces an abandoned/wrongly scoped one.
- `relates-to` for neighboring refactors sharing files but not strictly ordered.

CLI output requirements
=======================
Workflow plan must include impact-analysis checklist and safe rename guidance, not find-and-replace.

Pitfalls
========
Do not skip impact analysis. Do not use textual find-and-replace for symbol rename. Do not merge without reviewer PASS because tests alone are insufficient for refactor semantics.

title: Workflow registry entry: ambiguous-bug
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `ambiguous-bug` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when a symptom exists but likely cause, blast radius, or subsystem ownership is unclear. The first step is scoping, not patching.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. overthinker or explorer READ_ONLY scoping pass, depending on ambiguity type:
   - explorer for code-path/architecture mapping.
   - overthinker for tradeoff/risk decomposition.
2. debugger `--bead <bug-id> --context-depth 3 --keep-alive` to reproduce and isolate root cause.
3. executor or debugger resume for the minimal fix.
4. advisory passes when risk triggers apply.
5. reviewer with `--job <fix-job>`.
6. finalize + merge only after reviewer PASS.

Relationship edges:
- `discovered-from` from scoping bead to concrete bug/fix beads.
- `caused-by` from symptom/failure beads to root-cause bead.
- `validates` from reviewer/test/advisory beads to fix bead.
- `relates-to` for sibling hypotheses or competing suspected subsystems.
- `supersedes` if the initial broad bug bead is replaced by a narrower fix bead.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows suggest` should choose this workflow when prompt language includes unknown cause, broad failure surface, flake clusters, or "why is X failing?" without a known file/symbol.

Pitfalls
========
Overthinker output must be actionable: exact files/symbols/hypotheses. Escalate or re-scope if it returns a generic essay.

title: Workflow registry entry: clear-bug
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `clear-bug` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when the task is a bug with a reproducible symptom and bounded scope: one failing command/test, one likely code path, or one known subsystem. Cause still needs proof, but the bug is not architecture-ambiguous.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. debugger `--bead <bug-id> --background --keep-alive --context-depth 3` for repro/root cause first.
2. If debugger can safely patch in-place, resume debugger to apply minimal fix; otherwise spawn executor in the debugger/job workspace.
3. code-sanity/security-auditor only when triggers apply.
4. reviewer `--bead <review-id> --job <fix-job> --keep-alive`.
5. `sp finalize <review-job>` after PASS if needed, then `sp merge <fix-root-bead>`.

Relationship edges:
- `caused-by` from failing-test/repro bead to root-cause/fix bead when root cause is isolated.
- `validates` from reviewer/test/advisory beads to the fix bead.
- `discovered-from` for follow-up bugs found during diagnosis.
- `blocks` only for hard sequencing.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows show clear-bug` and `sp workflows plan` must print bash-ready `bd create`, `bd dep add --type`, `sp run`, `sp resume`, `sp finalize`, and `sp merge` examples.

Pitfalls
========
Do not spawn executor before debugger has root-caused. Do not close the bug bead until reviewer PASS. Do not use `blocks` for follow-ups or validation evidence.

title: [epic] Implement sp workflows executable workflow router
description:
Decision
========
Build this epic around an executable `sp workflows` command, not a prose-only workflow catalog.

Goal
====
Ship a canonical workflow router for Specialists. Given a task/bead/prompt, `sp workflows` should identify the right specialist chain, print the bead topology and relationship edge types, and emit bash-ready commands an orchestrator can run or inspect.

Primary CLI surface
===================
Minimum viable command set:

  sp workflows list
  sp workflows show <workflow-id>
  sp workflows suggest --bead <id>
  sp workflows suggest --prompt "<task description>"
  sp workflows plan --bead <id> --json

Later/explicit-mutation surface:

  sp workflows apply --bead <id> --dry-run
  sp workflows apply --bead <id>

Design direction
================
Use a data registry as the source of truth, not duplicated prose. Suggested layout:

  src/workflows/catalog.ts      # workflow definitions and matcher metadata
  src/cli/workflows.ts          # CLI rendering, suggest, plan, json
  docs/workflows.md             # generated or manually synced reference
  config/skills/using-specialists-v3/SKILL.md  # references `sp workflows` for routing

Every workflow entry must specify:
1. Trigger conditions / matching signals.
2. Specialist sequence and required flags (`--bead`, `--job`, `--keep-alive`, `--context-depth`, `--background`).
3. Bead topology and relationship edge types (`blocks`, `validates`, `discovered-from`, `caused-by`, `relates-to`, `supersedes`, etc.).
4. Output commands for the orchestrator to run or copy.
5. Termination, finalize, merge, and escalation criteria.
6. Common pitfalls.

Workflow entries to cover
=========================
- clear-bug
- ambiguous-bug
- new-feature
- refactor
- standalone-review
- test-generation
- doc-sync
- architecture-exploration
- security-audit

Acceptance
==========
- `sp workflows list` shows all workflow IDs with one-line triggers.
- `sp workflows show <id>` renders a full human-readable workflow entry.
- `sp workflows suggest --bead <id>` and `--prompt` choose a workflow with confidence/reasoning and do not mutate state.
- `sp workflows plan --bead <id> --json` emits structured commands, bead topology, edge types, and stop criteria.
- `using-specialists-v3` is updated to tell orchestrators to run `sp workflows suggest` when uncertain.
- Documentation stays secondary; the CLI registry is the canonical router.
- Relationship vocabulary from unitAI-ylphl.8 is used consistently instead of overloading `blocks`.

title: Migrate codebase to Zod 4
description: Dependabot PR #86 (zod ^4.4.3) fails tsc. Errors include removed/changed ZodEffects handling in src/cli/edit.ts, zod-to-json-schema/server schema type incompatibilities, and changed Zod issue codes/properties in src/specialist/schema.ts. Keep PR #86 closed/deferred until compatibility work is scoped.

title: Migrate test suite to Vitest 4 lockfile
description: OSV remediation for unitAI-4r9sf found package.json already requests Vitest 4.1.6, but syncing bun.lock fully to Vitest 4 causes the current test suite to fail (90 failed, 2 unhandled EBADF errors; notable changelog-keeper expectation drift and supervisor-sigterm append EBADF). The security PR uses osv-scanner.toml to temporarily acknowledge dev-only advisories from the stale Vitest 2 lock path while runtime dependency vulnerabilities are remediated. Remove those ignores after migrating tests and fully syncing bun.lock.
notes: Correction after final remediation: no osv-scanner.toml ignore is used; bun.lock was fully synced and OSV is clean. The follow-up remains for full test-suite failures observed under the synced Vitest 4 lock (bun run test: 90 failed, 2 EBADF unhandled errors), while lint/build/package CI paths pass.

title: Triage Semgrep findings from security baseline
description: Security pipeline bootstrap found 32 existing Semgrep audit findings during ./scripts/security-scan.sh, including path traversal / dynamic regexp / unsafe format string / local HTTP test warnings. Diff-only pre-push Semgrep passes for the bootstrap branch; triage full-scan findings before relying on scheduled Semgrep as a clean baseline.

title: Phase C: pi/specialists runtime artifact channel — write tool args bypass chat history for ledger writes
description:
## PROBLEM
Phase A delivers chunked file-backed ledger but tool-arg content of Write/Edit/Bash calls still goes into the conversation transcript that pi feeds back on each turn. With N=20-50 entries per chunk this is manageable, but at N=2000 the cumulative tool-arg cost across all chunks still grows linearly with N — Phase A is bounded-per-turn, not bounded-per-job. True N-invariance requires the pi/specialists runtime to support an 'artifact write' channel where the tool's input (the bytes being written to file) does NOT get echoed back into next-turn context, only an acknowledgment (path + size + sha256) does.

## SUCCESS
Specialists running with the new artifact-write protocol can write 2000 ledger rows across the run without those rows appearing in their own next-turn context window. The pi conversation history retains only the acknowledgment metadata for each write. Existing Edit/Write tool semantics remain unchanged for non-artifact writes (code edits etc continue to behave as before).

## SCOPE
- src/pi/session.ts and adjacent pi protocol layer — define the new tool variant (e.g. `write_artifact`, or a flag on existing write tools)
- src/specialist/runner.ts and supervisor.ts — propagate the artifact-write contract through to pi
- config/specialists/memory-processor.specialist.json — opt the spec into the artifact channel for ledger writes
- config/skills/memory-audit-transaction/SKILL.md — document the artifact channel + when to use it

## NON_GOALS
- No replacement of normal Edit/Write tools — artifact write is an addition, not a replacement
- No tool-result hiding for OTHER tool types (Read, Bash, etc) — only the write path
- Not a general 'context elision' feature — bounded scope: writing to known artifact paths only

## CONSTRAINTS
- Artifact-write must accept an explicit allowlist of paths (e.g. .tmp/memory-audit/*, .specialists/jobs/*) — cannot be used to silently elide arbitrary edits from history
- The full content is persisted to disk normally; only the conversation-history-echoing of that content is elided
- Acknowledgment in chat must include enough information for the agent to reason about: path, size, sha256, success/failure
- Backward compatible: specialists without artifact-channel support continue to use normal Write/Edit (Phase A fallback)
- A feature flag (env or spec-field) gates the artifact channel for incremental rollout

## VALIDATION
- [ ] Unit test: artifact write of 50KB content produces a chat-history record of <200 bytes (path + size + sha256)
- [ ] Disk verification: the 50KB content is fully written and readable
- [ ] Live re-dispatch of memory-processor on 2000 synthetic memories: context stays bounded regardless of N, ledger fully written
- [ ] Backward compatibility: an old specialist without artifact-channel still works via normal Write tool

## OUTPUT
- pi/specialists code changes with tests
- Updated memory-audit-transaction skill describing artifact-channel usage
- Live re-dispatch evidence on a synthetic large-N corpus
- Blast-radius analysis via gitnexus_impact on src/pi/session.ts before edits

title: Phase B: bd CLI surface for transactional memory audit (export --jsonl, forget --batch with dry-run + transaction + hash-guard + backup)
description:
## PROBLEM
Today, bd CLI exposes per-key `bd recall <key>` and per-key `bd forget <key>`. There is no bulk export with content hashes and no transactional batch forget with dry-run preview or rollback backup. Phase A works around this with shell loops, but loops are not atomic (interrupt mid-prune leaves partial state with no audit trail) and not hash-guarded (memory could mutate between classification and apply). Phase B closes those safety gaps by promoting the audit workflow into first-class bd commands.

## SUCCESS
bd CLI gains the following subcommands, all upstream-supported in the beads tool:
- `bd memories export --jsonl --full --content-hash --out <path>` — write every memory as one JSONL row per memory with sha256(content); supersedes the Phase A bash loop
- `bd forget --batch <ledger.jsonl> --dry-run --out <preview.json>` — produce a preview of what would be deleted, with hash-mismatch flags
- `bd forget --batch <ledger.jsonl> --apply --if-hash-matches --transaction --backup <backup.json> --log <apply.json>` — atomic apply; rolls back on any hash mismatch or partial failure; writes full backup before delete
- `bd memories restore --backup <backup.json>` — undo a batch apply from its backup file

## SCOPE
- bd tool source (beads repo, upstream). Specifically the bd memories and bd forget subcommand source files.
- Phase A's memory-audit-transaction skill updates the workflow to USE these new commands when available, with a Phase A bash-loop fallback when bd version is too old.

## NON_GOALS
- No changes to specialists or pi runtime in this phase
- No batch operation across other bd commands (close --batch, update --batch, etc) — out of scope, separate beads if desired
- No GUI/TUI integration

## CONSTRAINTS
- Atomic: the apply either deletes ALL eligible rows or NONE (transaction rollback on first failure)
- Hash-guarded: each ledger row's content_hash must match the live memory's content before delete; mismatches are reported in apply log and skipped
- Backup precedes delete: backup file is written and fsynced before the first delete
- The dry-run output is consumable by external tools (jq-friendly JSON)
- bd version bump (semver minor) to advertise the new surface

## VALIDATION
- [ ] bd memories export --jsonl on a 50-memory test corpus produces 50 rows with valid sha256 hashes
- [ ] bd forget --batch --dry-run on a ledger with 10 prune decisions produces a 10-entry preview with no actual deletes
- [ ] bd forget --batch --apply on the same ledger removes exactly 10 memories, writes backup, writes log
- [ ] Inject a hash-mismatch in the ledger → apply reports mismatch in log, skips that row, deletes others atomically
- [ ] Inject a forced mid-apply failure → apply rolls back, no memories deleted, backup intact
- [ ] bd memories restore --backup restores the 10 deleted memories

## OUTPUT
- Upstream bd PR(s) with the 4 new subcommands + tests
- Updated bd --version + release notes
- Phase A skill updated to consume new commands when bd version >= X, fallback to bash loop otherwise

title: Epic: memory-processor scaling — Transactional File-Backed Audit Ledger (Phases A/B/C)
description:
## PROBLEM
The memory-processor specialist cannot complete a full audit past ~150-200 bd memories. Two failure attempts: c791ef (deepseek, false 'all 507 current' at 82% context), fad36f (qwen with bulk-export, 105% context overflow into flailing). Root cause is structural: per-entry classification text + per-entry `bd forget` calls cumulate in chat history regardless of model context size. Overthinker analysis (job 70d764, bead unitAI-aofbp) proposed Transactional File-Backed Audit Ledger pattern with verdict SHIP, requiring changes across 4 layers: specialist spec, new skill, bd CLI surface, pi runtime artifact channel. The implementation is staged in three phases to bound risk and deliver value incrementally.

## SUCCESS
At epic close: memory-processor can complete a full audit of 2000+ memories without context overflow, with evidence-backed per-entry classifications, dry-run preview of pruning decisions, and atomic batch apply with rollback.

## SCOPE
- config/specialists/memory-processor.specialist.json (Phase A)
- config/skills/memory-audit-transaction/SKILL.md (Phase A, new file)
- bd CLI subcommands: memories export, forget --batch (Phase B, upstream beads tool)
- pi/specialists runtime artifact-channel write path (Phase C, src/pi/, src/specialist/)

## NON_GOALS
- No model swaps (qwen3.5-397b-a17b-thinking remains; pattern is model-agnostic)
- No split into multiple specialists — one memory-processor only

## CONSTRAINTS
- Conservative pruning rule preserved across all phases: every memory needs evidence, missing/low-confidence becomes Skipped not pruned
- Phase A must work today with no runtime changes (file-backed via standard Edit/Write/Bash tools)
- Phase B/C are upstream/runtime work; Phase A is the immediate fix
- Each phase's children must close before the next phase opens, except Phase B and Phase C can run in parallel after Phase A lands

## VALIDATION
- Phase A: re-dispatch memory-processor on this repo's 507 memories, observe context stays below 70%, ledger artifact written, .xtrm/memory.md regenerated, summary report contains evidence-backed per-entry classifications (sampled audit)
- Phase B: bd memories export --jsonl produces valid output; bd forget --batch --dry-run produces preview; bd forget --batch --apply --transaction --hash-guard atomically applies + writes backup
- Phase C: pi artifact channel verified to NOT echo content into chat history (transcript stays bounded with N)

## OUTPUT
Three child beads (one per phase), each with its own 7-section contract. This epic tracks parent.

title: Backport issue-contract guardrails into specialist orchestration docs and hooks
description:
## Problem

The future agent-native issue tracking contract board is deferred, but its core lesson should improve current specialist orchestration now: specialist job quality depends heavily on bead/issue contract quality.

Vague issues should not be dispatched to specialists without orchestrator review and repair.

## Scope

Backport lightweight issue-contract guardrails into existing surfaces:

- `config/skills/using-specialists*/SKILL.md` or active `using-specialists` skill source
- `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` guidance where appropriate
- optional Claude hook set that nudges on weak `bd create` / `bd update` / `sp run` usage
- optional mandatory-rule template for issue-contract expectations

## Desired behavior

Orchestrator should treat the bead as a job contract before dispatch:

1. Read bead/issue.
2. Check whether problem, desired outcome, scope, non-goals, acceptance criteria, validation, dependencies, risk, and suggested specialist routing are clear enough.
3. If not clear, improve the bead before launching specialists.
4. Do not send title-only or vague issues to executor/debugger/reviewer chains.
5. Map specialist outputs and reviewer evidence back to acceptance criteria.
6. File friction discovered during long runs as follow-up issues.

## Near-term hook ideas

Start as nudges, not hard blockers:

- On `bd create`: inspect new issue text and warn if it lacks acceptance criteria/validation/scope.
- On `bd update`: re-check when description or scope changes.
- On `sp run` / `specialists run`: warn or block if target bead is contract-weak.
- On session stop: warn about in-progress claimed issues without contract/readiness notes.

Hook output should be concise and give a suggested rewrite shape rather than generic scolding.

## Possible mandatory rule

```text
Before dispatching specialists, verify the bead is a usable job contract: clear problem, desired outcome, scope, non-goals, acceptance criteria, validation, dependencies, risk, and suggested chain. If missing, update the bead first. Do not launch executor/debugger/reviewer from title-only or vague issues.
```

## Relationship to deferred design

This is the immediate implementation path for ideas captured in:

- `docs/design/issuetracking.md`

Do not build the new issue tracking package as part of this task.

## Acceptance criteria

- `using-specialists` guidance includes an explicit issue-contract readiness step before dispatch.
- CLAUDE/AGENTS guidance for issue creation requires scoped, validation-backed implementation beads.
- Hook feasibility is assessed; if implemented, hooks nudge rather than hard block initially.
- Any new mandatory rule is compact and does not duplicate long planning docs.
- Documentation clearly says the full agent-native issue tracker is deferred.

title: Concurrent-write contention on shared dolt server post-0wz2p — design backpressure or serialization (mitigate xtrm-yb0u friction)
description:
## PROBLEM
Today's unitAI-0wz2p v2 fix (commit 7c2b630a) replaced per-worktree dolt servers with a symlink to common-root .beads/. Trade-off: pre-fix, N concurrent worktrees → N independent dolt servers (orphan leaks, but write contention distributed). Post-fix, N concurrent worktrees → all writes hit ONE shared server.

The 2026-05-09 xtrm-tools full-auto orchestration session (75 dispatches, 22 parallel chains) corrupted the bd Dolt journal at 02:48Z (offset 73333475). Required `dolt fsck --revive-journal-with-data-loss` recovery. ~30 min orchestrator stalled. xtrm-tools filed as xtrm-yb0u (bd-side bug). 

We need to decide whether our 0wz2p fix increases that risk class. Pre-fix the orchestrator's concurrent writes would have spread across N per-worktree dolts (rare collision); post-fix they all hit one server (frequent collision under load).

## DESIGN QUESTION
Under sustained N-worktree concurrent-bd-write load (e.g., 8+ parallel specialists with frequent bd close / bd kv set / auto-export), does the symlink-to-parent design hold? And if not, what's the mitigation?

Candidates:
1. **Backpressure**: bd commands invoked from worktrees serialize via a flock or local queue before hitting parent server.
2. **Batch mode**: switch dolt.auto-commit to 'batch'; flush only on session-end / explicit bd dolt commit.
3. **Read-mostly worktrees**: worktree bd ops route to a read-only replica or skip writes entirely; orchestrator owns all writes from main.
4. **Status quo + monitor**: track journal-corruption incidents in a bd memory; only act if recurrence rate justifies.

## SUCCESS
A documented decision based on observed frequency (sustained dispatch load) plus risk tolerance. If a mitigation is chosen, follow-up implementation bead.

## SCOPE
Decision-only bead. Implementation in follow-up.

## NON_GOALS
- Don't revert 0wz2p v2 — orphan-dolt leak was worse.
- Don't fix bd's journal robustness (upstream bd, xtrm-yb0u).

## CONSTRAINTS
- Decision should consider 75-dispatch / 22-chain orchestrator runs as the upper-end load, not the average.
- Backpressure adds latency to every bd call from worktrees; weigh vs corruption recovery cost (~30 min + data loss risk).

## VALIDATION
- Decision logged with reasoning + evidence from 2026-05-09 xtrm-tools session.
- If mitigation chosen, follow-up implementation bead is filed with measurable success criteria (e.g., zero journal corruptions under N-load test).

## CROSS-REFS
- xtrm-tools xtrm-yb0u (source friction; bd journal corruption)
- specialists unitAI-0wz2p (commit 7c2b630a — the symlink change that concentrated writes)
- 2026-05-09 xtrm-tools session report § 'Problems Encountered': 'Dolt journal corrupted mid-session at 02:48Z'

title: Auto-commit checkpoint hygiene: design squash-or-amend before reviewer hand-off (fix part of xtrm-ombq)
description:
## PROBLEM
auto_commit: checkpoint_on_waiting (live since Apr 13, 11e9b016) produces N intermediate commits per executor turn on the chain branch. Each is named `checkpoint(<role>): <bead> turn <N>`. By the time reviewer enters the worktree via --job, the branch has 3-15 such commits, polluting:
- Reviewer's git log view
- Cherry-pick at integration time (need to filter checkpoint-named commits manually — documented as a workaround in xtrm-tools using-specialists-v3 skill update proposal)
- The N-commit feature branch becomes the published commit history if not squashed at sp merge.

Today (fd60db04) we taught reviewer to live with multiple commits via system-prompt step 4. That's the operator-side workaround. Structural question:

## DESIGN QUESTION
Should the supervisor squash or amend checkpoint commits before the reviewer-hand-off moment (i.e., when status flips to 'waiting' and a reviewer dispatch via --job is expected)?

Three options:
1. **Status quo**: keep N commits; sp merge squashes at publish; reviewer prompt teaches multi-commit awareness. Pro: full audit trail in worktree. Con: pollution.
2. **Amend-on-resume**: when executor resumes via sp resume, amend the last checkpoint instead of adding new. N stays bounded. Pro: less pollution. Con: each resume rewrites recent history.
3. **Squash-before-handoff**: when status flips to 'waiting' and reviewer is incoming, squash all checkpoints since fork-base into one commit named `<role>(<bead>): <summary>`. Pro: clean N=1 branch for reviewer. Con: lose granular checkpoint trail; harder to revert specific turns.

## SUCCESS
A documented decision (this bead) chooses one of the three, with reasoning grounded in the 2026-05-09 xtrm-tools session data (~75 dispatches, 22 chains, 40-50% conflict rate at integration). If chosen path requires implementation, a follow-up implementation bead is filed.

## SCOPE
Decision-only bead. Implementation in follow-up.

## NON_GOALS
- Don't change auto-commit policy itself; only the post-checkpoint handling.
- Don't change sp merge squash behavior.

## CONSTRAINTS
- Reviewer must still see full work via cumulative-diff (covered separately by the axwq-mirror bead).
- Compatible with --force-stale-base / restitch flows.

## VALIDATION
- Operator decision logged in this bead.
- If squash-before-handoff or amend-on-resume chosen, follow-up implementation bead is filed.

## CROSS-REFS
- xtrm-tools xtrm-ombq (source friction)
- Today's commit fd60db04 (operator-side workaround in reviewer prompt)
- 2026-05-09 xtrm-tools session report § 'Architectural decisions': 'Squash-per-chain at integration time' is the current published-history strategy.

title: Deep eval: lessons + improvements from github.com/affaan-m/everything-claude-code
description:
## Goal
Comprehensive analysis of https://github.com/affaan-m/everything-claude-code
to identify patterns, hooks, skills, agent workflows, command surfaces, or
architecture choices that could materially improve our specialists +
xtrm-tools setup.

## Scope (focused on the linked repo only)
Repo URL: https://github.com/affaan-m/everything-claude-code

Read every: README, docs, hooks, skills, agents/specialists, commands,
settings.json patterns, MCP server configs, statusline scripts, install
scripts.

## Workflow
1. Clone to a scratch dir (e.g. /tmp/eval-eccc).
2. Spawn 3+ READ_ONLY explorers with disjoint scopes:
   - explorer A: hooks + scripts + automation
   - explorer B: skills + agents + commands
   - explorer C: docs + readme + architecture / philosophy
3. Synthesize via overthinker (CoT, --keep-alive) — what's borrowable,
   what's contradicting our current model, what's worth reproducing.
4. Optional researcher pass for any external libs/patterns referenced.
5. Output: a comparison matrix (their pattern vs ours, gap, action).

## Deliverable
A design doc (docs/design/eccc-eval.md or similar) listing:
- Patterns worth adopting (with bead spawned per item)
- Patterns we already do better
- Patterns we explicitly reject and why
- Open questions

## Constraints
- Specialists scoped *only* to that repo + obvious external refs.
- No edits to specialists/xtrm-tools as part of this eval — file follow-up
  beads for any concrete changes.

## Why
Cross-pollination from active claude-code community projects often
surfaces hook patterns, skill structures, or agent-orchestration tricks
we haven't found through internal iteration.

title: Live-proof: sp epic merge end-to-end
description: Throwaway epic to live-prove sp epic merge with multiple chains. Two child impl beads, each a tiny safe specialist config tweak. Demonstrates: (1) sp epic merge batches 2 chains; (2) per-chain finalize cascade fires; (3) topological order respected; (4) tsc gate passes after each merge; (5) epic header in sp ps shows derived readiness label (not persisted FAILED uppercase).

title: Model-validation guide: production-shape prompts, not smoke tests
description: PROBLEM: smoke prompts like 'summarize this paragraph' pass on many models. Production-shape Mercury Atomic Summarizer prompt (40KB content + role + JSON contract) reveals tool-call hallucinations on glm-5 and qwen3.5-thinking even with all isolation flags. Only kimi-k2.5 survives. Anyone selecting a new model from a smoke test will hit this in production. SUCCESS: docs/model-validation-checklist.md with a representative 30-40KB benchmark prompt, success criteria (canonical schema match, no <think>/<tool_call> tokens), failure-mode catalog. Optional: a 'sp model-validate <model> --bench <fixture>' subcommand. SCOPE: docs + optional CLI helper. NON_GOALS: no model gating in registry. REFERENCE: darth-feedor memory 'pi-llm-model-selection-for-darth-feedor-specialists'.

title: Document 'Option A' self-contained user-prompt pattern for consumers
description:
PROBLEM: until specialists-6vy + isolation flags landed, consumers couldn't trust spec.prompt.system to forward without contamination. Workaround that survived: load prompt locally, render '# ROLE
{system}

# TASK
{task_template}' with full variable substitution, send as variables.prompt with template='$prompt' and empty spec.prompt.system. Even after isolation flags landed, this remains the simplest seam — caller owns prompt assembly, no transport-time field splitting. SUCCESS: docs/consumer-prompt-patterns.md describing Option A (self-contained) vs Option B (system+user split) trade-offs and when each fits. Reference Python adapter from unitAI-huwov demonstrates A. SCOPE: docs only. NON_GOALS: no API change. REFERENCE: darth-feedor py_backend-bfr.

title: Test: SDK runner parity and benchmark harness
description:
## Covers

Implementation issues: unitAI-z2vpq.10, unitAI-z2vpq.11.

## Layer classification

Boundary + performance/integration: SDK runner, backend selection, service HTTP/CLI contract preservation.

## Strategy

Characterization tests for subprocess behavior, contract tests for SDK parity, benchmark harness for darth-feedor article/news payloads.

## Assertions

- SDK backend returns same `ScriptGenerateResult` envelope as subprocess backend.
- Zero tools/resource-discovery invariant is enforced.
- Backend selection defaults safely and is reversible.
- Benchmark captures latency, input tokens/overhead, schema drift, memory growth, and failure modes.

## Acceptance criteria

- SDK rollout cannot become default without benchmark evidence in bead notes.
- Subprocess fallback remains green.

title: Script/service runner: rollout SDK backend behind flag with subprocess fallback
description:
## Context

If SDK benchmark succeeds, service/script specialists need a safe rollout path that preserves subprocess fallback. If SDK is not suitable, RPC worker-pool feasibility should be captured as the alternate process-isolated path.

## What to build

- Add runner backend selection behind env/CLI flag (for example `SP_SCRIPT_RUNNER=sdk` or `sp serve --runner sdk`).
- Keep subprocess as fallback until production confidence is established.
- If SDK is rejected, create/attach decision notes for RPC worker-pool option instead of implementing it prematurely.
- Include migration docs for darth-feedor and other pipeline consumers.

## Acceptance criteria

- Backend selection is test-covered and defaults to subprocess until explicitly switched.
- SDK path (if enabled) preserves HTTP response contract and `sp script --json` envelope.
- Rollback path is one config change.
- Decision record captures SDK vs subprocess vs RPC pool tradeoffs.

title: SDK runner spike: zero-tool script specialist benchmark for darth-feedor workloads
description:
## Context

Medium-term migration. Pi docs recommend SDK for Node/TypeScript integrations. Service specialists are non-coding LLM transforms, so SDK can provide zero-tool in-memory sessions, exact ResourceLoader control, and lower per-request overhead than spawning Pi.

## What to build

- Implement a spike/prototype SDK runner path for script-class specialists without changing default production behavior.
- Enforce no coding tools and no resource discovery by default.
- Benchmark against subprocess runner on darth-feedor-style article/news summarization payloads.
- Compare latency, token input overhead, output schema drift, memory growth, auth/model behavior, and failure modes.

## Acceptance criteria

- Benchmark report attached to bead notes with subprocess vs SDK results.
- Prototype supports system prompt, rendered task prompt, model override, thinking level, timeout, JSON output parsing, and no tools.
- Clear go/no-go recommendation for production rollout.

title: Document script-class consumer contract: deploy recipe, prompt construction, model selection, integration patterns
description:
PROBLEM: specialists-service is shipping as the v1 production runtime for non-coding LLM tasks (darth-feedor cutover May 2026 was the first reference consumer). The consumer-side contract (HTTP adapter shape, JSON spec authoring, container deploy, prompt construction patterns, model selection trade-offs) is currently tribal knowledge — captured in darth-feedor session memories and friction reports but not codified upstream. Future Mercury pipelines and any external consumer needs a canonical doc.

SUCCESS:
(1) New doc at docs/script-class-consumer-guide.md (top-level next to specialists-service.md, authoring.md). Sections:
   - Overview: when to use script-class vs package-class
   - HTTP contract: POST /v1/generate request/response shape; required fields (specialist, variables, template, timeout_ms); error_type vocabulary; trace_id propagation
   - JSON spec authoring for script-class consumers: required execution flags, response_format choice (text vs json), model + fallback_model selection guidance with measured behaviour notes (e.g. instruction-tuned vs thinking models, kimi-k2.5 vs glm-5 vs qwen3.5-thinking observations from darth-feedor)
   - Prompt construction patterns:
     * Pattern A (self-contained user prompt; prompt.system empty) — recommended when consumer pre-renders templates; works with pi's current --no-* flag set
     * Pattern B (split: prompt.system for role/contract, task_template for content) — works once specialists-6vy lands
     * Pattern C (raw variables to service; task_template owns rendering) — design-doc pattern, requires consumer to pass typed variables; deferred until consumers refactor
   - Container deploy recipe: image (specialists-service:local), required user (1000:1000), HOME=/pi-home, mounts (project's .specialists rw, ~/.pi ro+rw for auth), healthcheck note (image lacks wget; use exec wget or remove healthcheck), network membership pattern
   - Observability: how to query observability.db; correlate trace_id with consumer logs; standard SLO for duration_ms / error_rate
   - Reference Python adapter: link to docs/examples/specialists_client.py; note error_type stability contract

(2) Update docs/specialists-service.md to cross-link to consumer-guide.md from §3 (request shape) and §5 (observability).

(3) Update docs/authoring.md script-class section to reference Pattern A/B/C and the model-selection guidance.

(4) Add a worked example: full lifecycle of mercury-atomic-summarizer (anonymized as 'newsletter-summarizer') from JSON spec → adapter call → pi spawn → observability row → consumer-side persistence. Pull from darth-feedor experience.

SCOPE: docs/ only. No code changes.

NON_GOALS: do not document the package-class runner (covered in existing specialist docs). Do not migrate or rewrite specialists-service.md; just add cross-links.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Doc must not assume readers come from xtrm/coding-agent context; assume general Python service author who knows HTTP+JSON.
- Model selection section must call out specialists-6vy as in-flight; once it lands, expand the recommended-models list (currently artificially constrained).
- Use measured numbers from darth-feedor where available (token counts, latencies, schema-match rates).

VALIDATION: a second engineer with no specialists-service prior context can build a Python consumer for an unrelated service in <1 day reading only this doc + adapter example.

OUTPUT: PR adding consumer-guide.md and cross-links. No runtime changes.

REFERENCE: discovered need during darth-feedor migration session 2026-05-05/06; complementary to specialists-37x and specialists-6vy.

title: Design task-scoped architecture snapshot injection for specialist runs
description: Specialists still begin too blind on fresh runs. Design a compact pre-run context surface that gives each specialist enough repo structure and semantic graph to orient quickly without injecting the whole codebase. Investigate a hybrid approach: lightweight directory/package map + task-scoped GitNexus architecture snapshot (context/clusters/processes/symbol pre-brief) derived from bead title/description/notes. The design should answer: (1) what static repo map is injected for every run, (2) what GitNexus data is queried per bead/run, (3) token budget and caching strategy, (4) fallback behavior when .gitnexus is missing/stale, (5) how this interacts with .xtrm/memory.md and context-depth, and (6) whether existing issues unitAI-tz4r and unitAI-231x should absorb or depend on this work. Deliverable is a concrete proposal, not implementation.

title: Workflow registry entry: architecture-exploration
description:
Purpose
=======
Define the `architecture-exploration` workflow entry for the `sp workflows` registry.

Trigger
=======
Use when the area is unfamiliar and there is not yet a concrete implementation/debugging task.

Registry entry must include
===========================
Sequence:
1. explorer READ_ONLY for codebase architecture/process mapping using GitNexus/Serena evidence.
2. overthinker only when the question is design tradeoff, risk critique, or premortem rather than code mapping.
3. Output recommends the next workflow: clear-bug, ambiguous-bug, new-feature, refactor, security-audit, or doc-sync.

Relationship edges:
- `discovered-from` from exploration bead to concrete follow-up workflow beads.
- `relates-to` for topical sibling explorations.
- `tracks` when local exploration mirrors an upstream/cross-project capability.
- Avoid `blocks` unless a later implementation truly cannot start before exploration completes.

CLI output requirements
=======================
`sp workflows suggest` should pick explorer for "how does this code work?" / unknown area prompts, and overthinker only for tradeoff/risk language.

Pitfalls
========
Do not jump to executor in an unfamiliar area. Do not use overthinker for straightforward code archaeology where explorer is a better fit.

title: Investigate xtrm-tools post-fix worktrees still spawning per-worktree dolt servers
description:
## Symptom
After unitAI-0wz2p / xtrm-as7d v2 (symlink) shipped at ~04:33 today, three
xtrm-tools worktrees created BETWEEN 05:21 and 05:42 STILL show real
.beads/ directories with their own dolt sql-server processes:

- xtrm-9xg2.1 (.git: 05:21:30, .beads: directory)
- xtrm-f8z7   (.git: 05:21:36, .beads: directory)
- xtrm-9xg2.2 (.git: 05:42:19, .beads: directory)

But fresh smokes after 06:00 (unitAI-bnsk7, xtrm-9ur8) correctly produce
`.beads -> <commonRoot>/.beads` symlinks. So the v2 code path works for
some new dispatches but was bypassed for the 05:21-05:42 cohort.

## Hypotheses (ranked)
1. **Stale tmux/shell session running pre-fix specialists** — supervisors
   started before 04:33 may have cached the old code in memory.
2. **Different worktree-creation path** that bypasses
   `provisionWorktree` (specialists) and `launchWorktreeSession` (xt).
3. **Race condition** between rebuild + dispatch.

## Investigation steps
- Check tmux ls in xtrm-tools at session start vs session close.
- grep all worktree-creation sites in specialists + xtrm-tools (already
  done partially during fix; redo with broader scope).
- Add a startup banner / meta event when provisionWorktree runs the
  symlink branch, so future audits can confirm code-path coverage.
- Repro: start a long-lived specialist before next code change, then ship
  a worktree-related fix, then dispatch new specialists from the
  pre-existing tmux — see if old worktrees still leak.

## Out of scope
- Reaping the 3 already-leaked worktrees (covered by sp clean reaper bead).

## Cross-refs
- unitAI-0wz2p, xtrm-as7d (the fixes)
- .xtrm/reports/2026-05-09-7b45dac0.md (today's session report)

title: Design: persistent devcontainer for all specialist + dev work (resource caps, identity isolation)
description:
## Question
Should all specialist + dev work move into a persistent, long-lived
docker container (devcontainer model) — entered via `docker exec` or
VS Code/Cursor attach — with bind-mounted source dirs, fixed memory/CPU
caps, and full toolchain (bun, node, dolt, pi, bd, gitnexus, serena,
pi-mcp-adapter) installed inside?

## Motivation
Today's pain:
- gitnexus / dolt / specialists OOM-eligible explosions hit the host
  workstation directly. No ceiling. Today's session caught 25 dolt
  servers, dozens of leaked gitnexus mcp / pi-coding-agent processes
  before fixes shipped (unitAI-ctl0o, unitAI-0wz2p).
- Specialist work runs as the same UID as the human dev. No identity
  separation; nothing stops a specialist from touching ~/.gnupg or
  similar. Memory rule docs ('blast radius') don't enforce.
- Tooling drift between sessions (bun/node/dolt versions) — host install
  is mutable.

## Proposed design (sketch)
- One long-lived container (e.g. `dev`), Debian or Ubuntu base
- Bind-mount: `~/dev` rw, `~/projects/mercury` rw, `~/.ssh` ro
- Resource caps: `--memory=24g --cpus=8` (tunable). OOM hits container,
  not host
- Container has its own UID (cleaner) or runs as host UID (simpler
  perms) — TBD
- Full toolchain pinned via Dockerfile: bun X, node Y, dolt Z, pi W,
  bd v1.0.3, gitnexus, serena, pi-mcp-adapter, pi-gitnexus, pi-serena-tools
- Persistent volumes for `.gitnexus/`, `.beads/`, npm cache, pi extension
  cache so re-entering is fast
- All worktrees, all dolt servers, all MCP processes live inside
- Host can still read specialist output via the bind mount

## Tradeoffs
Wins:
- Hard memory/CPU ceiling (current explosions confined)
- Clean OS + tooling pin (no host drift; reproducible)
- Identity separation (specialists can't write outside mounted dirs)
- Reversible — kill container, host stays clean
- Sets up future per-job container model if ever desired

Costs:
- One-day setup: reproducible install of pi-mcp-adapter / serena /
  gitnexus + their global node_module deps inside the container
- UID strategy decision (own-UID cleaner but bind-mount perms friction;
  host-UID simpler but loses one layer of isolation)
- All host integrations (clipboard, statusline, tmux history) move
  inside or get bridged
- VS Code / Cursor / Claude Code attach paths shift (devcontainer or
  docker exec from terminal)
- ~/.ssh mounted ro lets git push work but is a soft surface — OK for
  trusted dev work, less OK for untrusted specialists

## Cross-refs
- unitAI-ctl0o / unitAI-1phu7 (gitnexus mcp leak — would have been
  contained by container memory cap)
- unitAI-0wz2p (per-worktree dolt server leak — same)
- unitAI-c4g0m (LSP overhead, similar architecture-pattern bead)

## Decision needed
1. Do this now (one-day cost, get OOM ceiling immediately)?
2. Defer until next major architecture review?
3. Reject (current host-direct model is fine, keep iterating on
   per-component leak fixes)?

## Out of scope here
- Per-job ephemeral containers (separate, much larger lift —
  see priority comment in this bead's discussion thread)
- Forks-and-collaborators GitHub workflow (separate bead if pursued)

title: Design: compact tree sp ps default
description:
Initial design for reducing `sp ps` token/scroll noise without losing tree lineage.

Pain point:
- Current `sp ps` in repos with failed/large epics dumps many retained terminal jobs.
- Bead titles repeat per job row, wasting tokens and making agent context noisy.
- `sp ps -f` repaints the whole tree every second; in non-TTY/agent logs ANSI clear codes are not interpreted, so output looks duplicated/repainted and can hang/spam.
- But users and agents still need tree lineage: epic → prep → chain → worktree/branch → job. A flat dashboard loses important relationships.

Proposed direction:
- Keep the `sp ps` command family; avoid adding many new commands.
- Make default `sp ps` a compact tree, not a flat list and not the current fully-expanded history tree.
- Show active/running/waiting/actionable branches expanded.
- Collapse terminal leaves by default, but never silently: show counts and the newest/severest terminal job id, e.g. `… 5 terminal hidden: 2 error, 3 done · newest error abc123 · result abc123`.
- Show bead title once per bead group, not on every job row. Job rows can use `bead:<id>` only when needed.
- Keep ungrouped/legacy jobs in an `Ungrouped` section with the same compact rules.
- Add or refine inspect behavior under the same command: `sp ps <id>` should inspect job/epic/chain/bead when unambiguous; typed prefixes can resolve ambiguity: `job:<id>`, `epic:<id>`, `chain:<id>`, `bead:<id>`.
- Suggested flags: `sp ps` = compact tree; `--terminal` = include terminal leaves; `--full` = disable budget/truncation; `--all` remains backward-compatible noisy alias; `--json` remains full stable machine contract.
- `sp ps -f` should follow the compact tree only. If stdout is TTY, use TTY-safe rendering/alternate screen; if non-TTY, do not emit ANSI clear/cursor codes and either print one snapshot or concise timestamped snapshots.

Example target shape:

```text
sp ps · 2 epics · 4 active · 1 waiting · 17 terminal hidden · updated 14:32:10

EPIC unitAI-0mk8r  waiting  prep 1/1 done · chains 0/1 pass
  bead unitAI-0mk8r — Overthink sp ps compact tree UX
  prep
    ✓ abc123  overthinker  done  4m  8t·12tc
  chains
    chain main · root:unitAI-0mk8r · branch:feature/unitAI-0mk8r
      ▶ def456  reviewer     running  2m  ctx 41%  feed -f def456
      ? ghi789  implementer  waiting  9m  ctx 65%  resume ghi789 "..."
      … 5 terminal hidden: 2 error, 3 done · newest error jkl012 · result jkl012

Ungrouped
  branch main
    ▶ mno345  debugger  running  1m  ctx 12%  feed -f mno345

Use: sp ps <id> inspect · sp ps --terminal show hidden · sp ps --full disable budget · sp ps --json machine
```

Acceptance criteria:
- A design doc or implementation plan exists for compact tree `sp ps` rendering.
- Plan specifies default visibility, hidden terminal summary rules, title dedupe, inspect resolution, flags/backward compatibility, and follow/non-TTY behavior.
- Plan explicitly preserves epic/prep/chain/worktree/job lineage.
- Plan identifies migration risks for users expecting current verbose output.

title: session-close-report -> CHANGELOG SSOT verification across projects
description: PROBLEM: session-close-report Step 6 (added in xtrm-tools) mandates appending user-facing changes to CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] each session. Need to verify all consuming projects (specialists, mercury-darth-feedor, etc.) have synced versions of the skill and operators understand [Unreleased] is now the SSOT for next-version content. title: Sibling worktree visibility: dependent waves cannot run in parallel because each branches from main HEAD at spawn
description: Each worktree branches from main HEAD at spawn. Dependent beads cannot run in same wave because the second worktree won't see the first's commits. Operator workaround: merge phase 1 to main, then spawn phase 2. Architectural constraint, not a bug — but should be (a) documented prominently in using-specialists-v3 / planner output, (b) detected by stale-base guard or planner output as 'sequence these, do not parallelize', (c) considered for a 'sp wave' primitive. SCOPE: planner specialist, using-specialists-v3 skill, worktree spawn logic.

title: Test-issue dependency direction blocks impl close (inverse gate)
description: Planner created '.23 depends on .1' (test depends on impl). Closing .1 should be unblocked, but operator hit 'py_backend-thm.3 blocked by [py_backend-thm.23]' when closing .3. There is an inverse 'implementation can't close while test is open' gate (test-planning convention?) that contradicts the dep direction. Solution today: bd close --force. Need: clear rule for whether tests gate impl close, and which direction the edge should be.

title: Add sp result --payload flag for complete startup-payload breakdown
description:
PROBLEM: There is no first-class command to inspect the complete startup
payload breakdown of a specialist run. The data exists as a
`payload_breakdown` timeline event (every component name, tokens, bytes,
plus totals), but reaching it requires the awkward incantation:
`sp feed <job-id> --json | jq 'select(.type=="payload_breakdown")'`.
`sp ps <id>` shows aggregate kb/tokens only. `sp db stats --with-payload`
shows totals per job, no component breakdown. `sp result <id>` shows the
last turn but not the startup payload.

SUCCESS: `sp result <id> --payload` (or equivalent flag like
`--show-payload` / `sp payload <id>`) prints the complete payload breakdown
in human-readable form by default, with `--json` for machine-readable.
Default human format shows:
  - one summary line: total_kb, total_tokens, n components
  - sorted-descending component table: name, tokens, bytes, percentage
  - source attribution if available (e.g. system_prompt vs bead_context vs
    own/parent/blocker)

Works on any job with a recorded payload_breakdown event (running, waiting,
done, error). Errors gracefully when the event is missing (e.g. very old
jobs from before the payload-instrumentation epic).

SCOPE: src/cli/result.ts (or new src/cli/payload.ts if the second flag
makes more sense). Reads the payload_breakdown event from
events.jsonl/timeline_events DB. Tests in tests/unit/cli/.

NON_GOALS:
- No change to the underlying payload_breakdown event schema.
- No new aggregation across multiple jobs (use sp db stats --with-payload).
- No live streaming — one-shot read of the most recent payload_breakdown
  event for the given job.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Match the existing `sp result --json` flag shape if extending sp result.
- Reuse formatPayloadBreakdownSummary / formatPayloadBreakdownTopComponents
  from src/cli/feed.ts where possible.
- Stale-job tolerance: jobs predating the payload epic should report
  "no payload breakdown recorded" rather than crash.

VALIDATION:
- Unit tests: happy path on a recent job, missing-event path on a synthetic
  old job, --json format stable.
- Manual smoke: `sp result <recent-job-id> --payload` against a few
  job-ids from this repo's observability.db.
- using-kpi skill SKILL.md updated with a recipe pointer to the new flag
  (replaces the awkward jq one-liner).

OUTPUT: Diff summary, manual smoke output examples, follow-up risks.

DISCOVERED-FROM: Asked during gzrx/release-pipeline session 2026-04-30.
Current workaround: sp feed <id> --json | jq 'select(.type=="payload_breakdown")'.

title: Prototype Graphify-backed ScopeResolver for specialist startup scope_pack
description:
PROBLEM: Initial Graphify experiment in unitAI-kxeqz showed Graphify AST/query can cheaply surface relevant nodes/files for a scoped subset, but the public CLI full-build path is skill-driven and not ideal for SpecialistRunner integration. We need a small controlled prototype that calls Graphify Python modules or stripped local logic directly to produce a pre-specialist scope_pack.

SUCCESS: Implement or spike a provider-neutral ScopeResolver prototype that turns bead title/description into a compact scope_pack with candidate files/symbols/communities, then verifies candidates with GitNexus/Serena before prompt injection.

SCOPE:
- Do not depend on graphify install hooks.
- Prefer direct module calls / stripped AST extraction over shelling graphify <path>.
- Input: bead text plus optional known files.
- Output: JSON scope_pack: likely_files, likely_symbols, concepts, out_of_scope, confidence, evidence source.
- Add a small fixture/test using the gzrx/tool-catalog task and current known files.
- Keep behind an opt-in config flag; no default specialist behavior change.

NON-GOALS:
- No full repo graph commit.
- No replacement for GitNexus/Serena.
- No semantic LLM extraction unless explicitly enabled later.

VALIDATION:
- Prototype scope_pack identifies src/pi/session.ts and mapPermissionToTools for gzrx-like task text.
- GitNexus/Serena verification runs after Graphify candidate generation.
- Runtime/token costs documented.

DISCOVERED FROM: unitAI-kxeqz.
notes: Extend ScopeResolver prototype into unified TaskPrep: produce both graph scope query plan and FTS5 memory query plan. Use fast ~27B prep model only as JSON query planner/reranker; final outputs are verified scope_pack plus traceable compressed memory_pack. Compare/align with docs/design/shepherd.md: this is the narrow spawn-time implementation substrate for Shepherd-like routing/memory behavior, not the full always-on shepherd agent.

title: epic.4.upstream: Hot-tip injection for bd prime + gitnexus session-start (upstream PRs)
description:
PROBLEM: jhhu4.4 implemented version-pinned hot tips for sp's session-start hook (specialists e6176e29 + c421d0fd). bd's session-start surface is bd prime (the bd binary) and gitnexus's is its own hook — both are external/upstream and require PRs to those projects.

SUCCESS:
- bd prime emits a 'Hot Tips' subsection with ≤6 entries pinned to current bd version. Examples (from jhhu4.4):
  - bd create --parent <epic-id> auto-assigns .N
  - bd kv set 'memory-acked:<id>' before bd close
  - bd dep relate <a> <b> for non-blocking links
  - bd defer <id> +<duration> for hidden deferrals
  - bd query 'status=in_progress AND assignee=me' for SQL filters
- gitnexus session-start (or equivalent surface; current gitnexus-hook.cjs is PostToolUse-only — needs a SessionStart sibling) emits ≤3 hot tips:
  - gitnexus_impact BEFORE editing any symbol
  - gitnexus_detect_changes BEFORE every commit
  - gitnexus_rename for safe multi-file renames

SCOPE:
- Upstream beads project: bd prime output formatting
- Upstream gitnexus project: SessionStart hook (likely a new file; existing hook is PostToolUse)

NON_GOALS:
- No changes inside this repo — both surfaces live upstream.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Same constraints as jhhu4.4: ≤6 lines, ≤80 chars/entry, version-pinned header.
- Hot tips additive — must not displace existing prime content.

VALIDATION:
- After upstream releases land + 'npm i -g @beads/cli' (or equivalent), bd prime in any repo shows the Hot Tips block.
- Same for gitnexus.

OUTPUT: Two upstream PRs (one to beads, one to gitnexus). May be deferred indefinitely if upstream maintainers don't accept the pattern; that is acceptable since sp side already provides the version-pinned hot-tips functionality for the most-frequent agent friction surface.

title: sync-docs v3.1 tuning: canary C convergence (optional, low priority)
description:
PROBLEM: Canary C (unitAI-vjb99) showed sync-docs v3.1 makes correct edits but doesn't converge within prompt-level budget. Hits 600s hard timeout instead of finishing the loop. Model treats 'max 3 git show' as a literal-call cap on that specific op, then freely runs 60+ small grep/git-log verification calls within scope. The hard timeout caught it cleanly so no 9d3a-style runaway, and real correct edits land before the timeout — but invocations don't reliably emit a final report.

CONTEXT: Discovered during sync-docs v3.1 canary soak (2026-04-29). Canaries A/B/D PASS, C PARTIAL. v3.1 is shipped as-is — this is follow-up tuning, not a blocker.

OPTIONS (any ONE may suffice; not mutually exclusive):
1. Tighten prompt budget — replace per-op caps with a total-call cap, e.g. 'Total tool calls per run: ≤12 after Phase 1. After Phase 2 drift scan, decide UPDATED or NO_CHANGE_NEEDED within 8 more calls or emit BLOCKED: budget-exhausted.' Cheapest fix; no model risk.
2. Switch model — glm-5 is verification-happy. Try claude-haiku or claude-sonnet for decisiveness; fallback chain already routes to gemini-3-flash-preview on failure but doesn't help here (no failure, just slow). Run a head-to-head canary C across 3 models.
3. Schema-level max_tool_calls — overthinker re-critique (unitAI-9672u) flagged this as the only PARTIAL-CLOSED prior exploit. Would require supervisor-side feature: parse a spec field, enforce per-run.

SUGGESTED ORDER: 1 first (cheap, no risk), then 2 if still verbose, then 3 only if 1+2 insufficient.

NOT IN SCOPE: Re-running A/B/D canaries — those passed. No need to re-validate single-doc invariant or steer obedience.

EVIDENCE:
- Canary C job 6b38b1, bead unitAI-vjb99 — 35 turns / 72 tool calls / 9m16s before 10m timeout
- C made correct edits to docs/cli-reference.md before timeout (version bump, list-rules row, context-depth default fix)
- Re-critique: unitAI-9672u, job 7408a3 — overthinker SHIP_WITH_FIXES verdict
- v3.1 shipped artifacts: config/mandatory-rules/sync-docs-scope-discipline.md, config/specialists/sync-docs.specialist.json, .xtrm/skills/active/sync-docs/SKILL.md (+ .specialists/default mirrors)
